# Transcrição: 06- 31 Three Two One

- **Fonte:** 06- 31 Three Two One
- **Duração:** 11:54
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:00]

Okay, it's time to mine our past for some more stories. Look into the past and bring forth some of the things that we have forgotten over the years. This strategy is called 321 and I love it. 321 is predicated on the idea that if you were to ask me to tell a story about love right now, it would be very hard for me to find a new story about love. Love is a thing we think about all the time. It's a common prompt. If you ask me to tell a story about love, I'm probably going to tell you a story that I've told many times. Finding a new story about love would be very hard. 321 says, let's not use those prompts we use so often. Instead, let's take a laser pointer and look through our lives through the lens of a singular prompt, a word, a word that we've never really used before to look through our life. In doing so, maybe we'll find a new story. I'm going to play the game for you right now. I often play this game in workshops, but frankly, I play this game by myself all the time and I'll talk to you about that in a moment. Let's play the game. 321, the three is three prompts that I'm going to get in a moment. Three tangible, non-proper nouns. Tangible means you can touch it, so physical objects and non-proper, meaning scientist is a perfectly acceptable word to use. You can touch a scientist, but Benjamin Franklin is not because that's a proper noun. I'm going to get three tangible, non-proper nouns, three ways of looking at my life, three laser pointers that I can use to cast through my life looking for a story. The two is two minutes. I have about two minutes or less to tell you that story. Truthfully, I could speak to that topic for 20 seconds and be just fine, but the most time I have is about two minutes because I'm about to look at my life, find a new story, and tell it for the first time. I probably shouldn't speak more than two minutes. It's not going to be very crafty. It's not going to come out very well. Two minutes is what you get. One minute is how long you have to think about your story. I have one minute to look through my life through the lens of three words and find a story to tell. Three, two, one is also a great way to practice storytelling because it lowers the bar completely. If you have to find a story on the spot and you only get two minutes to tell it or less and you only get one minute to prepare it, nobody's going to tell a great story in three, two, one. The expectation of great storytelling is gone. Instead, the job is to find something to say.

## [03:00 - 06:03]

Find something that is mildly interesting to say. When I'm in a workshop, oftentimes I'll ask the participants to shout words at me. Right now, I'm going to use one of my favorite websites in the world, perchance.org. It's just a random object generator. There's many on the internet, but this is my favorite. It generates the most interesting words, an excellent set of words for storytelling. I'm going to turn to my computer right now and I'm going to hit the word randomize. When I do, it's going to show me three words. Hopefully, especially considering I'm recording this for you, hopefully at least one of these words will generate a new idea for a story. Let's see what happens. The three words I got were bananas, sticky note, and whip. I'm going to start with bananas and I'm thinking about my life through bananas, literally casting a laser pointer like arrow through my life and thinking about all the bananas that I've encountered along the way, thinking if there's a story somewhere and I just came up with one. I have an idea for a banana story that I kind of love. I'm not going to settle on that one though. I'll just check the other two first. So sticky note. I have a terrible sticky note story that I've already told, so I'll push that one aside, but it's a good one. Oh, and I have another sticky note story that just happened two days ago in my classroom. It's actually not in my homework for life. It didn't occur to me that it was worth writing down and suddenly I realized it should totally be in my homework for life. And then a whip story. I don't think I've ever had a whip in my life. I knew a girl in high school who we called whips and chains. Her real name was Michelle, but her nickname was whips and chains. I could actually tell a story about Michelle, who we called whips and chains, and that would qualify as a whip story too, but I think I'm going to go with the banana. The banana is the one that sort of brought a lot of joy to my heart. And it's a story that I just found, which is my favorite thing. I have just recovered the past and I'm pulling it forward. So I'm going to tell you my banana story right now. I'm standing in my classroom opening up a package of Pop Tarts. They're my daily snack. And I'm watching my students with their snacks. I'm watching one student in particular, a boy named Kyle, a boy I call Banana Boy. Because every day he brings bananas to school for his snack. And somehow, some way along the course of this year, I have taken to stealing my student's snacks and throwing them out the window of my classroom, out onto the grass. I don't really know how it started. I think it began with me taking a magic marker and writing on the oranges that my

## [06:03 - 09:04]

kids would bring to school. I'd write things like, please don't eat me on the skin of the orange so that they would have to peel the orange and then eat the orange. And I thought that was pretty funny. And then I started stealing Kyle's bananas and I would put them up in the light so that he couldn't reach them. And I'd torment him for a while and finally give him the banana back. And then I started opening the window and throwing the fruit out. I started rolling the oranges down the sidewalk towards the street. I had one girl, a girl named Riley, start bringing in more oranges because she thought it was so funny. Her mother finally said, why does Riley need five oranges every day? And I said, because I rolled them down and tried to get them into the street every day. And so with Kyle, what I eventually started doing was stealing his banana, opening the classroom door, the door to the outside, and throwing the banana at the tree, trying to get the banana to stick in the tree. And I have never been able to do it. And so on this day, I finish opening my pop tarts, I eat a pop tart, and then I see Kyle's banana on his desk and I walk over and I steal it. I steal it, but Kyle sort of lets me steal it now. It's just become the routine. It's what we do. And so I sweep by and I grab the banana and he pretends to be outraged. And he chases me to the door and I throw open the door and I toss the banana at the tree for the 83rd time and it gets stuck in the tree. It is a miraculous and magical moment. All of my students pour out the door. We surround the tree. We stare up into the tree at the banana stuck in the tree. This oak tree has now become a banana tree. We've been waiting for this moment all year long. None of us ever thought it would happen. And at last, it has happened. This is the stupid, incomprehensible, ridiculous things that teachers must do every day that they teach. As teachers, we must find ways to bring joy to our students. And oftentimes the best way to do that is in the most asinine way possible. I will never forget and Kyle will never forget. And those 24 kids, they will never forget the day I threw the banana into the tree. And at last, that banana stuck and stayed in that tree for three days. Thank you. So I love that. I just found a memory from the past that I don't think is in my homework for life. And I've recovered it. I've found it. I've brought it forward.

## [09:04 - 11:54]

I already think I might be able to tell that story on a stage someday. I'm at least going to tell it to my class. Probably tomorrow when I return to school, I'm going to tell my own children the story of banana boy. I may even try to find banana boy. He's probably in high school or maybe beyond high school. But I know his first and last name and in today's world, probably won't be hard for me to find Kyle the banana boy and remind him of this moment, except he doesn't need to be reminded. I promise you, there is no way he has forgotten that day. That's what 321 does. It allows you to look through your life through the lens of a banana. And find a moment that you can't believe you had forgotten. Just like these other moments, right? I had sticky note as one of my moments. I went back to two days ago and it occurred to me that a kid wrote something on a sticky note in my class to me. And I didn't enter that in homework for life. And now it occurs to me, I never want that to go. I put the sticky note in my drawer of special things. Why would I not also enter it into my homework for life? And Michelle, whips and chains, I had completely forgotten about Michelle, who we called whips and chains. Until I saw the word whip and I cast a laser pointer through my life, through the lens of whip. And I remembered a 17 year old girl who I knew when I was in high school, who used to meet me and all of our friends in the parking lot outside the grocery store where we would basically hang out for three hours. All of that came back to me using this simple strategy 321. We have to acknowledge that we leave a lot behind. We have to acknowledge that if we haven't been doing homework for life, we've been allowing our memories to slip through our fingers. 321 is a way of saying, let me go see what I've left behind. Let me see what I can find. Let me see what I can find through the use of a random word, which somehow always without fail allows us to find something. The story of Banana Boy was probably a two minute story. But sometimes I find something that's only 15 seconds or 30 seconds long. But those memories are just as good, just as meaningful, and oftentimes just as useful. They bring just as much joy to my heart as Banana Boy just brought back to me. 321 will allow you to reach into the past and bring it forward. So it's right here right now, back where it belongs.
